Parameters (26): Active, Search Mode, All, Max Blobs, Max Point Distance in Blob, Max Blob Movement, Area of Interest, Off, Center, Size, Rotate, Allow Movement Outside Area, Output Centroid, Output Velocity, Min Points per Blob, Blob Init Time (s), Lost Blob Timeout (s), Blob Movement Prediction Type, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index.

title: Blob Track TOP
text: Blob Track TOP (TOP). The Blob Track TOP is implemented using OpenCV. Parameters (40): Reset, Reset Pulse, Mono Source, Luminance, Draw Blob Bounds, Blob Bound Color, Red, Threshold, Minimum Blob Size, Maximum Blob Size, Maximum Move Distance, Delete Nearby Blobs, Minimum Distance, Delete Area Tolerance, Delete Overlapping Blobs, Delete Overlap Tolerance, Revive Blobs, Revive Time, Revive Area Difference, Revive Distance, Include Lost Blobs in Table, Include Expired Blobs in Table, Expired Time, Output Resolution.

title: Bloom TOP
text: Bloom TOP (TOP). The Bloom TOP creates a glow effect around bright parts of the input image that simulates light bouncing around a lens assembly of a camera. Parameters control how much of the bright spots of your input image get bloomed, how wide the bloom gets spread, and how it rolls off into the un-bloomed areas. Parameters (28): Pre-Black Level, Pre-Gamma, Pre-Brightness, Min Bloom Radius, Max Bloom Radius, Bloom Threshold, Bloom S-Curve, Bloom Fill, Bloom Intensity, Output, Input Image, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer.

title: Blur TOP
text: Blur TOP (TOP). The Blur TOP blurs the image with various kernel filters and radii. It can do multi-pass blurs and can do horizontal-only or vertical-only blurs. Parameters (29): Method, Horizontal and Vertical, Type, Catmull-Rom, Extend, Hold, Pre-Shrink, Filter Size, Sample Step, Sample Step Unit, Rotate Kernel, Dither, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel. Tips: Use Pre-Shrink to reduce resolution before blurring for better performance. Gaussian blur is the most common and natural-looking blur type. For real-time applications, keep blur size moderate - very large blurs are GPU-intensive. Warnings: Large blur sizes can significantly impact GPU performance. Consider using Pre-Shrink or lower resolution.

title: Body Track CHOP
text: Body Track CHOP (CHOP). OS: This operator is only supported under the Microsoft Windows operating system.Hardware: This operator uses the Augmented Reality (AR) SDK of the Nvidia Maxine system and requires a 20, 30, or 40 series Nvidia RTX GPU. Parameters (25): Active, Model Folder, TOP, High Performance, Bounding Boxes, Bounding Box Confidence, Keypoints, Keypoints Confidence, Rotations, Body 3D, Field of View (Horz), Aspect Correct UVs, Mirror U Positions, People Tracking, Max Bodies, Shadow Tracking Age, Probation Age, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root.

title: Bone COMP
text: Bone COMP (COMP). The Bone Component is the foundation of all of the Character Tools. It is a Component with most of the properties of a Geometry Component. It also has some extra features such as length, two types of geometry, end-to-end linking, and kinematic parameters. Parameters (77): Transform Order, Scale Rotate Translate, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Translate, X, Rotate, Scale, Pivot, Uniform Scale, Parent Transform Source, From Parent Object (Hierarchy), Parent Object, Look At, Forward Direction, +X, Look At Up Vector, Don't use up vector, Path SOP, Roll, Position, Orient along Path, Orient Up Vector, Auto-Bank Factor.

title: Bone Group SOP
text: Bone Group SOP (SOP). The Bone Group SOP groups primitives by common bones (shared bones). For more information regarding using Bone Groups for deforming geometry, see this article: Deforming_Geometry_(Skinning). All capture weights are re-normalized. Parameters (2): Max Bones per Point, Max Bones per Group.

title: Boolean SOP
text: Boolean SOP (SOP). The Boolean SOP takes two closed polygonal sets, A and B. Set these Sources to the SOPs with the 3D shapes that you wish to operate on. There are two important requirements for input geometry: Parameters (6): Operation, Union, Accurate Attributes Interpolation, Create Groups, Group A, Group B.

title: Box POP
text: Box POP (POP). The Box POP creates 6-sided boxes. The box can have 8 points - one at each corner with each face sharing a point with two other faces. Or the box can have 24 points with each of the 6 faces using 4 unique points (Unique Points toggle parameter). Where the box has 8 points, the user can choose to have vertex normals or primitive normals, which renders the cube "faceted" (flat-looking). Or the user can choose point normals (or no normals) which will make corners look non-faceted. The Connectivity menu allows for no primitives to be output (just points) (None), or the points connected with 4-point Quadrelaterals (quads) or 3-point Triangles, or output one Point Primitive for every point. The box can have optional rounded corners with control over radius and the amount of subdivision at the corners. Tip: To subdivide the faces send the Box POP to a [[Subdivide POP]] with Crease Weight 1. Texture coordinates can be created with different placement - per-face, or one cube map that is wrapped over all the faces, etc. Point or vertex colors can be applied. The Anchor parameters can shift the lower or upper side of the box to X=0, Y=0, Z=0. The box can be post-translated/rotated. Parameters (47): surftype, none, uniquepoints, modifybounds, size, sizex, scale, anchoru, anchorv, anchorw, cornertype, cornerradius, depth, t, tx, r, rx, normal, texture, mapping, boxinside, extenduv, color, bypass.

title: Box SOP
text: Box SOP (SOP). The Box SOP creates cuboids. These can be used as geometries by themselves, or they can be sub-divided for use with the Lattice SOP. Parameters (20): Orient Bounds, Modify Bounds, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Size, X, Center, Rotate, Scale, Reverse Anchors, Anchor U, Anchor V, Anchor W, Use Divisions, Divisions, Enforcement Bars, Consolidate Corner Points, Texture Coordinates, Off, Compute Normals.

title: Bridge SOP
text: Bridge SOP (SOP). The Bridge SOP is useful for skinning trimmed surfaces, holes, creating highly controllable joins between arms and body, branches or tube intersections. Parameters (16): Group, Bridge, All Primitives, N, Order, Min X-Sections, Use, The Frenet Frame of the Face, Circular Arc Fillet, Rotate Tangents, Scale  Tangents, Use Curvature, Scale Curvatures, Divisions per Span, Tolerance, Preserve Sharp Corners.

title: Bullet Solver CHOP
text: Bullet Solver CHOP (CHOP). The Bullet Solver CHOP is used in conjunction with a Bullet Dynamics system. It outputs the solved results from a Bullet simulation and can include the results for the entire system (Bullet Solver COMP) or an individual actor (Actor COMP) within a system. Parameters (18): Solver or Actor COMP, Transform Space, World, Collision Info, Translation, Rotation, Scale, Linear Velocity, Angular Velocity, Sample Rate, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: Bullet Solver COMP
text: Bullet Solver COMP (COMP). In a Bullet Dynamics system, the Bullet Solver COMP is analogous to the world/simulation in which actors/bodies (ie. Actor COMPs) operate. A Bullet Solver COMP contains any number of actors/bodies (Actor COMPs) or forces (Force COMP/Impulse Force COMP), and as the name suggests it also uses the Bullet Physics API to step through the simulation. Parameters (78): Actors, Global Forces, Gravitational Acceleration, X, Dimension, 1D, Linear Multiplier, Angular Multiplier, Initialize Sim and Collision Shapes, Initialize Sim, Start Sim, Play, Sample Rate, Simulation Speed, Feedback CHOP, Perform Contact Test, Always Simulate, Callbacks DAT, Transform Order, Scale Rotate Translate, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Translate, Rotate.

title: Button COMP
text: Button COMP (COMP). The Button Component is used in panels to provide interactive on/off buttons, including toggle buttons, momentary buttons, and sets of radio buttons or exclusive buttons. Radio and exclusive buttons act as a group, which can be as simple as all Button components in a Container component. The group can be specified more tightly using the Button Group Label parameter or the Button Group DAT parameter. Parameters (140): Label, Value, Button Type, Momentary, Button Group Label, Button Group DAT, Color, Red, X, Y, Width, Height, Fixed Aspect, Off, Aspect Ratio, Depth Layer, Horizontal Mode, Fixed Width, Left Anchor, Left Offset, Right Anchor, Right Offset, Horizontal Origin, Horizontal Fill Weight.

title: Cache Blend POP
text: Cache Blend POP (POP). The Cache Blend POP takes a reference to a [[Cache POP]] and outputs a blend of its cached data sets based on a Cache Index parameter and the Cache Weight parameter of the each of the sequential blocks. The blend is done on each point separately, so the results of one point is not affected by any other point. This is useful for doing time-blurs of geometry data, and calculating slope or speed of a point (assuming the number of points in the input is not changing). A Cache Index value of 0 blends the Cache POP's current frame's data, -1 blends in its previous cached data (the second newest set), and so on. The weights are normalized. See also: [[Cache POP]], [[Cache Select POP]], [[Blend POP]] Parameters (17): cachepop, cache, cache0index, cache0weight, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Cache POP, CHOP, Index Channel, Index Channel Unit, Weight Channel, Interpolate Frames, Cache.

title: Cache POP
text: Cache POP (POP). The Cache POP receives POP data every frame in its input and holds the most recent frames of data (up to Cache Size) in GPU memory. It outputs any of these held frames, determined by Output Index, which acts like a time-delay of its input. Furthermore, [[Cache Select POP]]s and [[Cache Blend POP]]s can access any combination the Cache POP's set of held inputs, allowing for multi-frame blends. If the Cache POP's Step Size is 3, for example, the Cache POP will hold every third frame, making the amount of time covered by the Cache POP triple. This reduces the amount of memory needed for long caches. Note that the data held in GPU memory is uncompressed. You can freeze the set of data held in the Cache POP by turning off the Active toggle parameter. You can clear and hold the cache to zero frames while the Reset parameter is on (the input is simply sent to the output), and you can hit Reset Pulse to clear the cache and start growing the cache again right away. (An [[Info CHOP]] connected to a Cache POP will show the number of frames in the cache.) The values you provide for Cache Index are 0 and negative integers. -2 is the third most recent cached data. Indexes that are out of range are clamped to be between and including -(Cache Size-1), and 0. You can also specify two channels to define the weights, one for weight and for indices. Cache POP gives you choices on how to number the index of the cached images. When the .toe or .tox file is restarted, the cache starts cleared - nothing is saved in the file. Note that when frames are dropped, data in the cache may be older that they would be if frames were not dropped. The [[Trail POP]], though different in functionality, deals with frame drops more elegantly by filling in data for missing frames, and tagging each timeslice with an Age attribute. The Cache POP does not have this built-in. See also [[Cache Select POP]], [[Cache Blend POP]], [[Trail POP]], [[Cache TOP]], [[Texture 3D TOP]]. Parameters (24): .toe, active, cachesize, cachesizeunit, seconds, step, stepunit, outputindex, outputindexunit, reset, resetpulse, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Active, Always Cook, Cache Size, Step Size, Output Index, Interpolate Frames, Reset.

title: Cache Select POP
text: Cache Select POP (POP). The Cache Select POP takes a reference to a Cache POP and outputs one of its cached data sets based on the Cache Index parameter. A Cache Index value of 0 outputs the Cache POP's current frame's data, -1 outputs its previous cached data (the second newest set), and so on. So the values you provide for index are negative integers and 0. Indexes that are out of range are clamped to be the Cache POP's -(Cache Size-1), and 0. See also: [[Cache POP]], [[Cache Blend POP]] Parameters (11): cachepop, index, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Cache POP, Cache Index, Interpolate Frames.

title: Cache Select TOP
text: Cache Select TOP (TOP). The Cache Select TOP grabs an image from a Cache TOP based on the index parameter. This gives direct, random access to any image stored in a Cache TOP.The Cache Select TOP grabs an image from a Cache TOP based on the index parameter. This gives direct, random access to any image stored in a Cache TOP. Parameters (19): Cache TOP, Cache Index, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask, Pixel Format.

title: Cache SOP
text: Cache SOP (SOP). The Cache SOP collects its input geometry in a cache for faster random-access playback of multiple SOPs. It should be used when cook times for a chain of SOPs is long and a quicker playback is needed. Parameters (9): Active, Pre-Fill, Cache Size, Step Size, Output Index, Cache Points Only, Blend Position, Reset, Reset Pulse.

title: Cache TOP
text: Cache TOP (TOP). The Cache TOP stores a sequence of images into GPU memory. These cached images can be read by the graphics card much faster than an image cache in main memory or reading images off disk. Parameters (32): Active, Active Pulse, Get One Image on Startup, Replace Single, Replace Pulse, Replace Index, Pre-Fill, Pre-Fill Pulse, Cache Size, Step Size, Output Index, Output Index Unit, Interpolate Frames, Always Cook, Reset, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1.

title: Camera Blend COMP
text: Camera Blend COMP (COMP). The Camera Blend Component blends the 3D object transforms and viewing settings of multiple Camera Components together. It gives you some extra flexibility in setting up parent-child relationships. It operates like the Switch SOP and Sequence Blend SOPs insofar as it takes more than one input and blends or switches those into one output. Parameters (102): Type, Blend, Sequence, Reset Frame, Weight 1, Mask 1, Weight 2, Mask 2, Weight 3, Mask 3, Weight 4, Mask 4, Normal Offset, Orient Axes, Short Rotation, Transform Order, Scale Rotate Translate, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Translate, X, Rotate, Scale, Pivot.

title: Camera COMP
text: Camera COMP (COMP). The Camera COMP is a 3D scene object that defines the viewpoint and projection used when rendering a 3D scene with a Render TOP. It behaves like a real-world camera: it has position (Translate), orientation (Rotate), field-of-view (FOV), near/far clip planes, and optional look-at target. The Render TOP's Camera parameter must be set to the Camera COMP's path to use it. Multiple Camera COMPs can coexist in a scene, and different Render TOPs can each reference a different camera. Parameters (7): Translate, Rotate, Look At, Projection, FOV, Near, Far. Related: Render TOP, Geometry COMP, Light COMP, Object CHOP, Camera Blend COMP, Null COMP, Constraint COMP. Tips: The Camera COMP is referenced by path in the Render TOP Camera parameter — it is NOT wire-connected. Drive Translate and Rotate parameters from CHOP expressions for smooth animated camera movement: op('null_cam')['tx'] Use the Look At parameter to automatically orient the camera toward a target object without manually computing rotation. Warnings: If the Render TOP Camera parameter is empty or points to a non-existent COMP, the render output will be black. Changing the camera's projection type between perspective and orthographic mid-animation causes a discontinuous jump.

title: Cap SOP
text: Cap SOP (SOP). The Cap SOP is used to close open areas with flat or rounded coverings. Meshes are capped by extending the mesh in either the U or V direction (e.g. a NURBS tube). Faces are capped by appending a separate face or hull cap. Parameters (10): Group, Preserve NURB Shape U, First U Cap, No End Cap, Divisions, Scale, Last U Cap, Preserve NURB Shape V, First V Cap, Last V Cap.

title: Capture Region SOP
text: Capture Region SOP (SOP). The Capture Region SOP defines capture region (cregion), which is a type of primitive which can be thought of as a modified tube primitive (a tube with half a sphere on either end). The hemispheres on the ends of the tubes are called "caps". Like any other primitive, each Capture Region has a primitive number and can be assigned primitive attributes. A Capture Region is simply a volume which is used to define the point capture weighting for a geometry. It is then animated to deform the geometry. Parameters (11): Orientation, X Axis, Center, X, Top Height, Top Cap, Bottom Height, Bottom Cap, Max/Min Weight, Display Color, Red.

title: Capture SOP
text: Capture SOP (SOP). The Capture SOP is used to weight points in a geometry to capture regions. The weighting scheme is described in the next section, Capture Region SOP. Parameters (12): Group, Hierarchy, Weight from, Surface, Capture Frame, Point Coloring, Default Source Color, Override File, Save File, Increment Save File, Save All Data to File, Save Selected Points to File.

title: Carve SOP
text: Carve SOP (SOP). The Carve SOP works with any face or surface type, be that polygon, Bzier, or NURBS. It can be used to slice a primitive, cut it into multiple sections, or extract points or cross-sections from it. Parameters (18): Group, First U, Second U, First V, Second V, Carve Method, Cut, Keep Inside, Keep Outside, Extract Type, Extract 3D Isoparametric Curve(s), Keep Original, Location, Divisions, U Divisions, V Divisions, Cut at All Internal U Breakpoints, Cut at All Internal V Breakpoints.

title: Channel Mix TOP
text: Channel Mix TOP (TOP). The Channel Mix TOP allows mixing of the input RGBA channels to any other color channel of the output. For example, the pixels in the blue channel of the input can be added to the output's red channel, added or subtracted by the amount in Red parameter's blue column. Parameters (22): Red, Green, Blue, Alpha, Constant, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask, Pixel Format.

title: CHOP Execute DAT
text: CHOP Execute DAT (CHOP). The CHOP Execute DAT will run its script when the channel values of a specified CHOP change. You can specify which channels to look at, and trigger based on their values changing in various ways.  The script gets called for every sample that changes, so when rendering one frame, it may get called 2 or more times per channel, depending on how many frames forward TouchDesigner has stepped (see Time Slicing). Parameters (26): Active, Execute from, Current Node, From Operator, CHOP, Channel, Off to On, While On, On to Off, While Off, Value Change, While Off/On Frequency, Execute For Every Sample, Edit.., File, Sync to File, Load on Start, Load File, Write on Toe Save, Write File, Language, Input, Edit/View Extension, dat.

title: CHOP to DAT
text: CHOP to DAT (CHOP). The CHOP to DAT allows you to get CHOP channel values into a DAT in table format. Parameters (11): CHOP, Include Names, Latest Sample when Time Slice, Output, Row per Channel, Language, Input, Edit/View Extension, dat, Custom Extension, Word Wrap.

title: CHOP to POP
text: CHOP to POP (POP). The CHOP to POP takes CHOP channels and lets you convert them to attributes of a POP. Each CHOP sample becomes one POP point. You can manually list the CHOP channels you want to convert, and then list the names of the POP attributes you want create, matching channels to attributes one by one. Built-in attributes like P, N, Color and Tex are assumed to be their multi-component forms. Other channel names will be assumed to be converted to scalar float attributes. Channel to attribute mapping can be done through sequential blocks which give you more control over the type of attribute you create, for example, an array of 5 integers. Alternately, channels can be automatically converted to attributes based on a naming standard. Set the Channels Selection menu to Autoconvert Precise Names. This is the same channel naming standard that [[POP to CHOP]] produces when POP to CHOP's Name Format menu is set to Precise, which handles attributes that have () and []: * the attribute name is the same including maintaining capital letters * components of a vector attribute have _0 _1 _2 suffixed i.e. Tex_0 Tex_1 Tex_2 * [[Array Attribute]]s are create with channel names with a leading-following underscore - MyArray_0_. For example, channel names Abc_0_ and Abc_1_ create an array attribute named Abc with 2 float elements. * for vector arrays MyVectorArray_0_1. * The channel name optionally has a single character at the end to signify data type: f (float), F (double), i int, I double int, u unsigned, U double unsigned. You can get the P position attribute from the CHOP, or you can create your own line for P with a start-end position that you specify. The POP points can be connected as an open or closed linestrip, or be created as single-point primitives, or have no primitives. You can override the number of points that it produces and CHOP to POP will optionally interpolate from samples of the input. See also [[POP to CHOP]] Parameters (45): P, chop, surftype, none, overridenumpoints, numpoints, interpolate, specifypos, startpos, startposx, endpos, endposx, closed, chanscope, attrscope, attr, attr0chanscope, attr0name, custom, attr0customname, attr0type, float, attr0numcomps, 1.

title: CHOP to SOP
text: CHOP to SOP (CHOP). The CHOP to SOP takes CHOP channels and generates 3D polygons in a SOP. It reads sample data from a CHOP and converts it into point positions and point attributes. This makes it complementary to the SOP to CHOP. The Channels created by the SOP to CHOP can be modified and then re-inserted into the SOP network via a CHOP to SOP. Parameters (14): Group, CHOP, Start Position, X, End Position, Method, Static, Channel Scope, Attribute Scope, Organize by Attribute, Mapping, One Sample to Each Point, Compute Normals, Compute Tangents.

title: CHOP to TOP
text: CHOP to TOP (CHOP). The CHOP to TOP puts CHOP channels into a TOP image. By default the texture created will be 32-bit floating point to match the precision of the CHOP data. This can be changed setting the TOP's Pixel Format to something other than 'Input'. Parameters (25): CHOP, Data Format, R, Clamp CHOP Values, Image Layout, Row per Channel Set (Cropped), Extra Pixel Values (RGBA), Red, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask.

title: Chroma Key TOP
text: Chroma Key TOP (TOP). The Chroma Key TOP pulls a key matte from the image using Hue, Saturation, and Value settings. If a pixel falls between the Min and Max parameters for all three settings, then it is included in the key. It outputs an alpha map, or the image with the key color removed. Parameters (34): Hue Min, Hue Max, Hue Soft Low, Hue Soft High, Sat Min, Sat Max, Sat Soft Low, Sat Soft High, Val Min, Val Max, Val Soft Low, Val Soft High, Invert New Alpha, RGB Output, Source RGB * New Alpha, Alpha Output, New Alpha, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect.

title: Circle POP
text: Circle POP (POP). The Circle POP creates a number of points in a circle, ellipse or arc, and optionally connects them as a line strip, a set of triangles, separate 2-point lines, or unconnected as point primitives. You can choose to not Close the circle, where the last point/section is not connected to first. You can add texture coordinate, normal or tangent attributes to the points or to the vertices. Of note are normals, which can optionally point radially away from the center, or tangent to the circle. Orientation can be in any of the XY, YZ or ZX planes and then further rotated and translated.m If a POP is connected to the input, it will translate / scale the circle to the bounding box of the input. If a POP is connected to the input and if Modify Bounds is set, it will scale / translate the circle to the bounding box of the input, and you can further translate/scale/rotate the circle. See also [[Rectangle POP]], [[Sphere POP]], [[Primitive POP]], [[Line POP]]. Parameters (47): connectivity, none, orient, xy, modifybounds, rad, radx, scale, divs, closed, angle, beginangle, anchoru, anchorv, t, tx, r, rx, texture, normal, normaldirection, default, tangent, tangentdirection.

title: Circle SOP
text: Circle SOP (SOP). The Circle SOP creates open or closed arcs, circles and ellipses. Parameters (20): Primitive Type, Primitive, Orientation, XY Plane, Modify Bounds, Radius, X, Center, Reverse Anchors, Anchor U, Anchor V, Order, Divisions, Arc Type, Closed, Arc Angles, Imperfect, Texture Coordinates, Off, Compute Normals.

title: Circle TOP
text: Circle TOP (TOP). The Circle TOP can be used to generate circles, ellipses and N-sided polygons. Parameters (48): Radius, X, Radius Unit, Rotate, Center, Center Unit, Justify Horizontal, Left, Justify Vertical, Bottom, Fill Color, Red, Fill Alpha, Border Color, Border Alpha, Background Color, Background Alpha, Multiply RGB by Alpha, Border Width, Border Width Unit, Border Offset, Arc Angles, Anti-Alias, Softness.

title: Clay SOP
text: Clay SOP (SOP). The Clay SOP deforms faces and surfaces by pulling points that lie directly on them. As opposed to the Point SOP or other SOPs that manipulate control points (CVs), the Clay SOP operates on the primitive contours themselves, providing a direct, intuitive, and unconstrained way of reshaping geometry. Thus, rather than translating CVs to change the aspect of the primitive, the Clay SOP takes the inverse approach of manipulating the primitive's skin to reposition the CVs. Parameters (25): Group, Warp Method, Matrix, Transform Order, Scale Rotate Translate, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Translate, X, Rotate, Scale, Pivot, Distance, Normal, Direction, Coordinates, U and V, Deform along U, U, U Bias, U Sharpness, Deform along V, V, V Bias.

title: Clip Blender CHOP
text: Clip Blender CHOP (CHOP). License: Only available in TouchDesigner Pro. Parameters (40): Default Clip CHOP, DAT List, Target, X, Play Speed, Step Forward, Step Backward, A End Script, Delay Samples, Reset, Reset Pulse, Output Time Remaining, Time Channel, X Root Trans, Y Root Trans, Z Root Trans, X Root Rot, Y Root Rot, Z Root Rot, Queue Enable, Queue Trigger, Pre-Rotate Adds, Transform Next Clip, Translate.

title: Clip CHOP
text: Clip CHOP (CHOP). License: Only available in TouchDesigner Pro. Parameters (28): Rule DAT, Callbacks DAT, Reload DATs, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Transition, Blend, Blend Time, Blend Time Unit, Next Clip, Next Blend Time, Next Blend Time Unit, Loop While Active, Loop Release Rule, Index Channel, Position Type, Override / Absolute, Rotation Type, Same as Position, Pause at End, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate.

title: Clip DAT
text: Clip DAT (DAT). License: Only available in TouchDesigner Pro. Parameters (17): Edit.., File, Reload File, Execute from, Current Node, Clip, Component, Execute on Frame (First), Execute on Frame (Loop), Execute on Exit, Print State, Language, Input, Edit/View Extension, dat, Custom Extension, Word Wrap.

title: Clip SOP
text: Clip SOP (SOP). The Clip SOP cuts and creases source geometry with a plane. Parameters (9): Group, Keep, Primitives Above the Plane, Distance, Direction, X, Create Groups, Above Plane, Below Plane.

title: Clock CHOP
text: Clock CHOP (CHOP). The Clock CHOP generates channels that reflect the time of year, month, week, day, hour, minute, second and millisecond. It also has a moon cycle channel. It provides the date as separate channels and in different units. It presents the time in two ways: Parameters (37): Output, Units, Hour Format, 12 hour, Hour Adjust, Start Reference, Since Jan 1 2000, Millisecond, Second, Minute, Hour, AM/PM, Day of Week, Day of Month, Day of Year, Week, Month, Year, Latitude, NS, North, Longitude, EW, East.

title: Composite CHOP
text: Composite CHOP (CHOP). The Composite CHOP layers (blends) the channels of one CHOP on the channels of another CHOP. The first input is the base input and the second is the layer input. It is designed for blending static multi-frame motion channels. Blending time-sliced channels or single-frame channels should be done with the Blend CHOP. Parameters (29): Base Hold, Match by, Channel Number, Quaternion Blend, Shortest Path Rotation Blending, Rotation Scope, Cycle Length, Effect, Unit Values, Absolute, Start, Start Unit, Peak, Peak Unit, Release, Release Unit, End, End Unit, Rise Shape, Linear, Fall Shape, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match.

title: Composite TOP
text: Composite TOP (TOP). The Composite TOP is a multi-input TOP that will perform a composite operation for each input. Select the composite operation using the Operation parameter on the Composite parameter page. Parameters (44): TOP, Preview Grid, Select Input, Input Index, Operation, Add, Swap Operation Order, Fixed Layer, Input 1, Pre-Fit Overlay, Fill, Justify Horizontal, Left, Justify Vertical, Bottom, Extend Overlay, Hold, Rotate, Translate, X, Translate Units, Scale, Pivot, Pivot Units. Tips: Use different operation modes (Over, Add, Multiply, Screen) for various compositing effects. The 'Over' operation is standard alpha compositing. Multiple inputs are composited in order - first input is the bottom layer.

title: Connectivity POP
text: Connectivity POP (POP). The Connectivity POP is intended to re-connect the input's points with a set of new primitives, similar to what the [[Grid POP]] or [[Torus POP]] does to their sets of points. It works on any POP, especially those that ore organized as multi-[[Dimension]]s, that is, having rows, columns, slices and any number of dimensions. Like the Grid POP you can connect the points in any dimension with line strips, or in any two dimensions with meshes of triangles or quads, or leave them as point primitives or no primitives. It generally takes care of the incoming POP's arrangements points, and also offers more kinds of line strip connections, like zig-zag or spiral connections of points. See also [[Torus POP]], [[Dimension POP]], [[Dimension]], [[Grid POP]], [[Plane POP]] Parameters (6): Connectivity, (First) Dimension Index, Second Dimension Index, (First) Dimension Closed, Second Dimension Closed, Reorder Points by Prim/Vertex Order.

title: Constant CHOP
text: Constant CHOP (CHOP). The Constant CHOP creates new constant-value channels. Each channel can be named and assigned a different value. To create a channel, simply enter a channel name in a name parameter on the Constant page, then adjust the below value. Parameters (25): Constant, Name, Value, Snapshot Input, First Channel, Active Needs Current, Single Sample, Start, Start Unit, I, End, End Unit, Sample Rate, Extend Left, Hold, Extend Right, Default Value, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root. Tips: Use Constant CHOP to define named channels with static values for parameter driving. Great as a starting point for expressions and exports. Name channels to match target parameters for easy auto-export (e.g., 'tx', 'ty', 'tz').

title: Constant MAT
text: Constant MAT (MAT). The Constant MAT renders a constant color on a material. Parameters (57): Apply Projection Maps, Color, Red, Alpha, Apply Point Color, Color Map, Extend U, Hold, Extend V, Extend W, Filter, Nearest, Anisotropic Filter, Off, Texture Coord, Texture Layer 0 (uv[0-2]), Coord Interpolation, Perspective Correct, Deform, Get Bone Data:, From a SOP, SOP with Capture Data, pCaptPath Attrib, pCaptData Attrib.

title: Constant TOP
text: Constant TOP (TOP). The Constant TOP sets the red, green, blue, and alpha (r, g, b, and a) channels individually. It is commonly used to create a solid color TOP image. Parameters (27): Color, Red, Alpha, Multiply RGB by Alpha., RGBA Units, 0 to 1, Comp Over Input, Operation, Add, Swap Order, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness.

title: Constraint COMP
text: Constraint COMP (COMP). A Constraint COMP is used to restrict the movement of the bodies in a set of Actor COMPs. Currently this can be done in a few ways: point to point, hinge or slider. Constraints can either be applied on a single body or between two bodies. Constraints can be used to create connectivity between bodies, or to create bodies that can move but need to have that movement restricted in some way. Some examples of constraints in the real world: a train, a door, an arm. Parameters (43): Active, Type, Point To Point, Body to Body, Collisions between Bodies, Display Constraint, Actor COMP, Actor Bodies, Pivot, X, Hinge Axis, Slider Rotation, Enable Limits, Lower Linear Limit, Upper Linear Limit, Lower Angular Limit, Upper Angular Limit, Extension, Object, Name, Promote, Re-Init Extensions, Parent Shortcut, Global OP Shortcut.

title: Container COMP
text: Container COMP (COMP). Container COMP operator for TouchDesigner Parameters (133): X, Y, Width, Height, Fixed Aspect, Off, Aspect Ratio, Depth Layer, Horizontal Mode, Fixed Width, Left Anchor, Left Offset, Right Anchor, Right Offset, Horizontal Origin, Horizontal Fill Weight, Vertical Mode, Fixed Height, Bottom Anchor, Bottom Offset, Top Anchor, Top Offset, Vertical Origin, Vertical Fill Weight.

title: Convert DAT
text: Convert DAT (DAT). The Convert DAT changes the text format from simple text to table form and vice-versa. Parameters (11): How, To Text, Remove Blank Lines, Split Cells at, Concatenate with, Language, Input, Edit/View Extension, dat, Custom Extension, Word Wrap.

title: Convert POP
text: Convert POP (POP). The Convert POP is a general utility that keeps the points of the input, but reconnects them in various ways as primitives. This includes converting to and from line strips, opening/closing line strips, converting quads to triangles, stripping off all primitives, and creating Point Primitives from points. It can also “flatten” a POP into an orderly form by first making the points of all primitives unique, and then re-ordering all the points by going through each primitive and ordering the points according to the vertices of each primitive (menu item Unique Points and Reorder to Prim/Vertex Order). The first point in the point list is the first vertex of the first primitive, and the last point of the point list is the last vertex of the last primitive. See also [[Primitive POP]]. Parameters (9): convert, topointprims, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Convert.

title: Convert SOP
text: Convert SOP (SOP). The Convert SOP converts geometry from one geometry type to another type. Types include polygon, mesh, Bezier patche, particle and sphere primitive. Parameters (16): Group, From Type, All Types, Convert to, Polygon, Connectivity, Rows, U, V, Trim-Curve, U Order, V Order, Preserve Original, Interpolate Through Hulls, Particle Type, Render as Lines.

title: Convolve TOP
text: Convolve TOP (TOP). The Convolve TOP uses a DAT table containing numeric coefficients. For each pixel, it combines its RGBA values and it's neighboring pixels' RGBA values by multiplying the values by the corresponding coefficients in the table, adding the results together. For example, if the table is 5 rows and 5 columns, the pixel and its 24 neighbors are combined. Parameters (21): Coefficients DAT, Normalize Coefficients, Apply to Alpha, Offset, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask, Pixel Format.

title: Copy CHOP
text: Copy CHOP (CHOP). The Copy CHOP produces multiple copies of the second input along the timeline of the first input. The first input provides the trigger signals or the convolve levels. Parameters (20): Copy Method, Triggered Copy, Output Method, One Channel per Template Channel, Trigger Threshold, Remainder, Discard Remainder, Keep Non-Scoped Channels, Stamp Inputs, Copy, Param, Value, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: Copy POP
text: Copy POP (POP). The Copy POP makes copies of its input using (1) the Number of Copies parameter that specifies the number of copies to make with the transform applied to each copy, and (2) a second-input Template POP where a copy is placed at each point of the template. For each copy in (1), the transforms are cumulative, that is, the transform is applied to the transform of the previous copy, with no transform applied to the first copy. Once (1) is done, then (2) is applied to the result. The Template page chooses which attributes of the template are used to define the transform for each copy-per-template point. The Template Attributes page determines what attributes are added to the output, and how the attributes are combinations of the first input and the template input, multiplying or adding them together. If the number of copies is greater than 1, The Dimension of the first input is increased by one, and the number of dimensions is increased by the second (template) input's dimension. See [[Dimension]]. See also: [[GLSL Copy POP]]. Parameters (94): ncy, xord, srt, rord, xyz, t, tx, r, rx, s, sx, p, px, scale, copyid, off, copyidname, lookat, upvector, upvectorx, forwarddir, posx, vlength, dimension.

title: Copy SOP
text: Copy SOP (SOP). The Copy SOP lets you make copies of the geometry of other SOPs and apply a transformation to each copy. Parameters (39): Source Group, Template Group, Number of Copies, Primitives per Point, Rotate to Normal, Transform Cumulative, Transform Order, Scale Rotate Translate, Rotate Order, Rx Ry Rz, Translate, X, Rotate, Scale, Pivot, Uniform Scale, Normals Maintain Length, Create Output Groups, Copy Groups, Look At, Up Vector, Stamp Inputs, Copy, Param.

title: Corner Pin TOP
text: Corner Pin TOP (TOP). The Corner Pin TOP can perform two operations. The Extract page lets you specify a sub-section of the image to use by moving 4 points. The Corner Pin page let you move the corner points of the extracted image to any location. Parameters (31): Bottom Left, Bottom Left Unit, Bottom Right, Bottom Right Unit, Top Left, Top Left Unit, Top Right, Top Right Unit, Extend, Hold, Grid Refinement, Background Color, Red, Pre-Multiply RGB by Alpha, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu.

title: Count CHOP
text: Count CHOP (CHOP). The Count CHOP counts the number of times a channel crosses a trigger or release threshold. It operates in either static or realtime ("Cook to Current Frame") mode. Parameters (28): Release = Trigger Threshold, Trigger Threshold, Release Threshold, Re-Trigger Delay, Re-Trigger Delay Unit, Trigger On, Increasing Values, Limit, Off, Limit Minimum, Limit Maximum, Off to On, None, While On, On to Off, While Off, Reset Condition, Reset Value, Reset, Reset Pulse, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate.

title: CPlusPlus CHOP
text: CPlusPlus CHOP (CHOP). The CPlusPlus CHOP allows you to make custom CHOP operators by writing your own plugin using C++. Parameters (11): Plugin Path, Re-Init Class, Unload Plugin, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: CPlusPlus DAT
text: CPlusPlus DAT (DAT). The CPlusPlus DAT allows you to make custom DAT operators by writing your own plugin using C++. Parameters (9): Plugin Path, Re-Init Class, Unload Plugin, Language, Input, Edit/View Extension, dat, Custom Extension, Word Wrap.

title: CPlusPlus POP
text: CPlusPlus POP (POP). The CPlusPlus POP allows you to make custom POP operators by writing your own plugin using C++. See [[Write a CPlusPlus Plugin]], [[Write a CPlusPlus POP]] and the other articles in the [[:Category:C++ | C++ Category]] for more detailed information on how to make plugins for use with this node and how to access example projects. Parameters (12): plugin, reinit, unloadplugin, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Plugin Path, Re-Init Class, Unload Plugin.

title: CPlusPlus SOP
text: CPlusPlus SOP (SOP). The CPlusPlus SOP allows you to make custom SOP operators by writing your own plugin using C++. Parameters (3): Plugin Path, Re-Init Class, Unload Plugin.

title: CPlusPlus TOP
text: CPlusPlus TOP (TOP). The CPlusPlus TOP allows you to make custom TOP operators by writing your own plugin using C++. Parameters (26): Plugin Path, Re-Init Class, Unload Plugin, Anti-Alias, 1x, Depth Buffer, None, Stencil Buffer, # of Color Buffers, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes.

title: Creep SOP
text: Creep SOP (SOP). The Creep SOP lets you deform and animate Source Input (input0) geometry along the surface of the Path Input (input1) geometry. Parameters (7): Reset, Reset Method, Fill path, Translate, X, Rotate, Scale.

title: Crop TOP
text: Crop TOP (TOP). The Crop TOP crops an image by defining the position of the left, right, bottom, and top edges of the image. The cropped part of the image is discarded, thus reducing the resolution of the image. Parameters (27): Crop Left, Crop Left Unit, Crop Right, Crop Right Unit, Crop Bottom, Crop Bottom Unit, Crop Top, Crop Top Unit, Extend, Hold, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness.

title: Cross CHOP
text: Cross CHOP (CHOP). The Cross CHOP is a multi input OP that blends between 2 inputs at a time. This is similar to a Switch CHOP however the Cross CHOP allows for interpolation between the inputs. Parameters (9): Cross, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: Cross TOP

Parameters (32): Cross, Fixed Layer, Input 1, Pre-Fit Overlay, Fill, Extend Overlay, Hold, Rotate, Translate, X, Translate Unit, Scale, Pivot, Pivot Unit, Legacy Transform, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1.

title: Cube Map TOP
text: Cube Map TOP (TOP). The Cube Map TOP builds a texture map in the Cube Map internal texture format. It accepts a vertical cross image, or 1 input per side of the cube. The Phong MAT can use the cube maps from the Cube Map TOP for reflections. The Render TOP can also create cube maps. You can sample a cube map in a GLSL shader by declaring them like this Parameters (19): Mode, One Input Per Side to Cube Map, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask, Pixel Format.

title: Curve POP
text: Curve POP (POP). The Curve POP is used to generate a curve in XY (Z=0) that can be used as a lookup curve for a [[Lookup Attribute POP]] or elsewhere. It is a "function" in that the P(0) X-value steps forward uniformly from point to point, while the P(1) Y-value takes on different interpolated shapes. The curve it generates is also in an attribute Curve, which you can use instead of P(1) for clarity. Like the [[S Curve CHOP]], it defaults to an ease-in ease-out curve, but the Curve POP lets you add multiple segments (sequential blocks) that enable a greater range of curves. The curve can be made of several segments defined by [[Sequential Blocks]] of parameters, each with its own start and end values and Curve Type. The Curve Type includes Constant, Linear, the Eases and Bezier. The Alpha and Beta values affect the curve, where Alpha makes it more- or less-sloped, and Beta makes it more biased to the start or end of the segment. When Bezier is selected, Slope In and Slope Out affect the slopes at the end-points of the segment. More generally, we treat the 2 axes as U and V. The curves can output as points-only, a linestrip, or as an RGBA color curves. Because the U values can start and end at any value, you can normalize the curve so the U values (P(0)) are between 0 and 1. The curve can be extended to double length and made symmetric using the Symmetric toggle. The Lookup page lets you apply the curve as a lookup without first converting anything to points. This preerves maximum accuracy, and avoids interpolation problems when a lookup curve sharply steps from one value to another as may occur between two segments. In this case you attach an input POP where you want to modify one or more attributes with the curve. In this mode it behaves similar to the [[Lookup Attribute POP]]. Tip: If you want specific points to exactly hit your U keyframe values, make sure your (Number of Points - 1) is divisible evenly by your U values. See also [[S Curve CHOP]], [[Line POP]], [[Lookup Attribute POP]], [[Pattern POP]] Parameters (62): P(0), totallength, outputcurve, points, normalizeu, symmetric, parsize, 1, startu, seg, seg0type, constant, seg0u, seg0specstartval, seg0startval, seg0endval, seg0alpha, seg0beta, seg0slopein, seg0slopeout, applylookup, lookupindexattr, fromlow, fromhigh.

title: Curveclay SOP
text:
Curveclay SOP (SOP). The Curveclay SOP is similar to the Clay SOP in that you deform a spline surface not by modifying the CVs but by directly manipulating the surface. However, instead of using a point on the surface, you use one or more faces to deform that surface. Also, CurveClay does not yet support polygonal meshes. 		
The combination of inputs will determine the modes of transformation. For any combination of inputs, the following parameters modify the following behaviors of the SOP. Parameters (12): Face Group, Surface Group, Divisions on Face, Sharpness, Refinement, Projection Axis, X, Displacement Axis, Surface Normal, Distance, Deform Inside of Loop, Consider Profiles Individually.

title: Curvesect SOP
text: Curvesect SOP (SOP). The Curvesect SOP finds the intersections or the points of minimum distance between two or more faces (polygons, Bziers, and NURBS curves) or between faces and a polygonal or spline surface. Parameters (13): Face Group, Cutter Group, Find All Intersections, Tolerance, Method, Cut, Left Face Pieces, Keep All, Right Face Pieces, Affect, Left Input, Extract Point, Keep Original.

title: Cycle CHOP
text: Cycle CHOP (CHOP). The Cycle CHOP creates cycles. It can repeat the channels any number of times before and after the original. It can also make a single cycle have a smooth transition from its end to its beginning, so it loops smoothly. Parameters (21): Cycles Before, Cycles After, Mirror Cycles, Blend Start to End, Method, Preserve Length, Shape, Linear, Region, Blend Region Units, Bias, Step, Step Scope, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: DAT to POP
text: DAT to POP (POP). The DAT to POP converts a DAT table into a POP point list, where each column is a name heading followed by values, one row per POP point. The table must be structured as you get with POP to DAT, though for DAT to POP you don't need an index column as it is automatically implied. The column headers need to be in the usual POP form. Vectors (like float3) must be expressed as one column per component of the vector, with headings, for example, P(0) or Color(3). Arrays components must be in MyArray[0] etc form. In each [[Sequential_Parameters#Sequence_Blocks|sequential block]], you need to specify an attribute to create. You can use ? in P(?) to specify that columns P(0), P(1) and P(2) will form a float3 vector P. At present, DAT to POP cannot convert or specify primitives or vertices of primitives. This will we implemented at a later date, at which time you will be able to represent any POP as three or four DATs. Parameters (23): index, pointsdat, attr, attr0columnstype, name, attr0columns, attr0outputscope, P, attr0overrideauto, attr0type, float, attr0numcomps, 1, attr0isarray, attr0arraysize, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Points DAT, New Attribute.

title: Deform SOP
text: Deform SOP (SOP). The Deform SOP takes geometry along with point weights (assigned by the Capture SOP) and deforms geometry as Capture Regions are moved. This gives you the flexibility to procedurally modify geometry between the Capture and Deform SOPs. Parameters (5): Group, Delete Capture Attributes, Delete Point Colors, Deform Normals, Skeleton Root Path.

title: Delay CHOP

Parameters (12): Delay, Delay Unit, Max Delay, Max Delay Unit, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: Delete CHOP
text: Delete CHOP (CHOP). The Delete CHOP removes entire channels and/or individual samples of its input. Parameters (32): Delete Channels, Delete, Scoped Channels, Select Channels, By Channel Name, Channel Names, Channel Numbers, Channel Value, Off, Value Range, Select Constant Valued Channels, Delete Samples, Channels to Compare, Use First Channel, Multi-Compare Channels, Any Chan in Range, Delete Condition, Less Than Value 1, Value 1, Include Value 1, Value 2, Include Value 2, Delete Compare Channels, One Sample if All Deleted.

title: Delete POP
text: Delete POP (POP). The Delete POP removes (or keeps) points or primitives using 5 methods found on the Attribute, Thin, Pattern, Group and Bounding pages. You can choose to delete points or primitives. On the Attribute page you can delete based on the values of point or primitive attributes. On the Thin page you can delete based on parameter-defined ranges, steps or randomly. You can delete based on patterns using [[Pattern Matching]] on the Pattern page. You can delete based on point or primitive groups on the Group page. You can delete based on bounding ranges on any attribute on the Bounding page. Four of the methods let you build the logic using sequential blocks of parameters. For example on the Attribute page, one block selects P(0) > 0 and another that selects P(1) < 0 and ANDed together they will delete points in a quarter of the space. Otherwise is common to build an attribute in a [[Math Mix POP]] and use it in the Delete POP. When deleting primitives, if points or vertices are no longer used, they are deleted. When deleting points, the vertices and primitives that use them can be deleted in part or entirely. Deleted points can cause like strips to be split, trimmed or deleted entirely. For pattern matching on the Pattern page, see [[Pattern Matching]]. Note: Deleting attributes can be done with the [[Attribute POP]], the [[Select POP]] and all the Math POPs. See also [[Group POP]]. The [[Field POP]] also can delete points. Parameters (67): P(0) > 0, invert, dele, entity, primitive, linestripbehavior, delpointoflinestrip, cpureadback, attr, attr0combine, and, attr0inattr, attr0func, lt, attr0value, attr0invert, thinenabled, thinoutrange, thinrangestart, thinrangelength, thinstep, thinrandom, thinrandomseed, thininvert.

The Delete SOP deletes input geometry as selected by a group specification or a geometry selection by using either of the three selection options: by entity number, by a bounding volume, and by entity (primitive/point) normals. You can choose to delete the selected or the non-selected geometry. title: Depth TOP
text: Depth TOP (TOP). The Depth TOP reads an image containing depth information from a scene described in a specified Render TOP. The resulting image is black (0) at pixels where the surface is at the near depth value (Camera's parameter "Near"). It is white (1) at pixels where the surface is at the far depth value (parameter "Far"). title: Dimension POP
text: Dimension POP (POP). The Dimension POP can alter the dimensions of a POP. See [[Dimension]]. The product of the new dimensions has to be equal to the number of points, otherwise it will give a warning and not change anything. In all cases the points and point order does not change in any way, nor do vertices nor primitives – no data change. It only changes how the organization of points is viewed. You can add a Dimension <syntaxhighlight>20 33 -> 20 33 1 or 1 20 33</syntaxhighlight> Or remove a dimension <syntaxhighlight>12 5 30 -> 60 39</syntaxhighlight> Or swap, which often would not make sense <syntaxhighlight>12 25 -> 25 12</syntaxhighlight> Or split a dimension into two equal dimensions <syntaxhighlight>160 5 15 -> 80 2 5 15</syntaxhighlight> Parameters (11): dim, dim0number, bypass, delinputattrs, parmcolorspace, srgb, parmreferencewhite, default, Mode, Dimension Order, Dimension.

title: Direct Display Out TOP
text: Direct Display Out TOP (TOP). The Direct Display Out TOP uses new technology available in Vulkan which allows directly outputting the DisplayPort/HDMI ports on the GPU, without interacting with the Windows Desktop. This avoids the need for a Window COMP, avoids cases where other windows and the mouse cursor will go in front of the content window, and avoids a lot of the issues caused by the Windows Compositor, as described Perfect Playback. Up to 3 outputs can be controlled via Direct Display. Parameters (20): Active, Display, Hardware Frame-Lock, Output Resolution, Use Input, Resolution, W, Resolution Menu, Use Global Res Multiplier, Output Aspect, Aspect, Aspect1, Aspect Menu, Input Smoothness, Nearest Pixel, Fill Viewer, Viewer Smoothness, Passes, Channel Mask, Pixel Format.

title: Divide SOP
text: Divide SOP (SOP). The Divide SOP divides incoming polygonal geometry. It will smooth input polygons, dividing polygons, as well as sub-divide input polygons using the Bricker option. Bricker is also useful for polygon faces with more than four edges to chop them up into quads and triangles allowing for proper shading when using deformation tools. Parameters (14): Group, Convex Polygons, Maximum Edges, Triangulate Non-Planar, Smooth Polygons, Weight, Divisions, Bricker Polygons, Size, X, Offset, Angle, Remove Shared Edges, Compute Dual.

title: DMX Fixture POP
text: DMX Fixture POP (POP). The DMX Fixture POP constructs DMX universes from a POP input, and is used in conjunction with a [[DMX Out POP]] to send DMX to a device. In the POP input, each primitive is analogous to a fixture. Each fixture is iterated over and the DMX profile defined on the Channels page is used to construct the DMX universes for each fixture. The fixture/primitives are iterated over in the order they're defined in the POP. The following primitive attributes, as unsigned integers, can be used to specify net, subnet, universe, channel, and netaddress, allowing for per-fixture customization without needing to use the Routing Table: * DMXFixtureNet * DMXFixtureSubnet * DMXFixtureUniverse * DMXFixtureChannel * DMXFixtureNetAddress additional to this, an unsigned integer attribute DMXFixtureGap can be used to overwrite the Channel Gap parameter per primitive in a DMX Fixture POP. The [[DMX Map DAT]] can be used to inspect the DMX universes created. === Creating the DMX Profile === The DMX Profile is defined by the Channel sequence on the DMX Profile page. DMX Channels are sequenced in the universe output in the same order they're defined on the DMX Profile page. For any given Channel sequence, all attribute components will be interleaved together. Eg. 'P' will be interleaved as 'Px Py Pz'. If the DMX profile requires them to not be interleaved then they will instead need to be separated into their own Channel sequence blocks. Multiple attributes can be specified together: Eg. 'P Color' will interleave P and Color together like so: 'Px Py Pz Colorr Colorg Colorb Colora'. If the interleaved components are of different resolutions, then they will need to be separated into their own Channel sequence blocks, but by enabling Merge with Above Block they can be interleaved together. For Point Value Types, all points will be iterated over before the next Channel Sequence. Individual attribute components can also be specified. Eg. 'P(0) P(1)' to use just position x and y. Attribute components are interleaved by value unless Interleave Bytes is enabled for the attribute grouping. Example: Position attribute P with 16-bit resolution will be interleaved as Px[0] Px[1] Py[0] Py[1] Pz[0] Pz[1] ... when the Interleave Bytes toggle is disabled, but interleave as Px[0] Py[0] Pz[0] Px[1] Py[1] Pz[1] ... when the Interleave Bytes toggle is enabled. === Auto Layout Mode === The DMX Fixture POP is in Auto Layout mode when the Auto Layout toggle is enabled, or as a default when the toggle is disabled and no routing table is referenced, or the routing table has no rows. Auto Layout mode auto-increments channel and universe values from a defined starting point (using the net, subnet, universe, and channel parameters). Primitives are iterated over in primitive order (eg. prim 0 is first) and each primitive's points are iterated over in point order (eg. point 0 is first). The DMX Profile page defines the channel layout for each primitive. It is executed once per primitive, and then a channel gap is added (specified by parameter) between each primitive channel layout. If Quantize Universe is set to off, then the next primitive will begin immediately after the channel gap. But if Quantize Universe is set to By Fixture, it will first check to see if the next primitive (ie. fixture) can fit in the remaining channels of the universe, and if not, it will jump to the next universe at channel=1. Similarly, if Quantize Universe is set to By Components, and a multi-component DMX Channel entry on the DMX Profile page cannot fit all components in the remaining channels of the universe (eg. P has 3 components x y z so with 8-bit resolution would require 3 remaining channels), then it will jump to the next universe at channel=1. When the DMXFixtureNetAddress attribute is set, each specific net address value will have their own independently incrementing channel values. Eg. if there are 8 primitives each of exactly 512 channels, and there are 4 with DMXFixtureNetAddress=192.168.0.1 and 4 with DMXFixtureNetAddress=192.168.0.2, then the resulting channel layout will be 4 universes [0-3] with netaddress=192.168.0.1 and 4 universes [0-3] with netaddress=192.168.0.2. When the DMXFixtureUniverse attribute is set, the channel value is set to 1 for that universe, unless DMXFixtureChannel is also set. If the DMXFixtureUniverse is greater than 15 then the DMXFixtureNet and DMXFixtureSubnet attribute values will not be used if set. See also: [[DMX Out POP]], [[DMX Map DAT]], [[DMX]] Parameters (43): DMXFixtureNet, active, autolayout, routingtable, net, subnet, universe, channel, changap, quantizeuni, dmxchan, dmxchan0name, dmxchan0valuetype, point, dmxchan0valuesource, constant, dmxchan0group, dmxchan0numcomps, 1, dmxchan0value, dmxchan0valuer, dmxchan0attr, dmxchan0valueres, b8.

title: DMX In CHOP
text: DMX In CHOP (CHOP). The DMX In CHOP receives channels from DMX, Art-Net, sACN or KiNET devices. Channel values for DMX are 0-255. Note that input rate is limited to the DMX maximum refresh rate of 44Hz. Parameters (29): Active, Interface, Enttec Generic Serial, KiNET Version, DmxOut (v1), Device, Serial Port, Format, Packet Per Sample (Timesliced), Art-Net Net (0-127), Art-Net Subnet (0-15), Art-Net Universe (0-15), Filter Table, Net Name, Subnet Name, Universe Name, KiNET Port Name, Start Codes, Multicast, Queue Size, Rate, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match.

title: DMX Map DAT
text: DMX Map DAT (DAT). The DMX Map DAT references a DMX Out POP or a DMX Fixture POP and outputs their DMX universes in table format. The DMX Map DAT is useful for visualizing DMX universe layouts and in the case of the DMX Out POP it can be helpful for debugging channel conflicts between DMX Fixture POPs.

title: DMX Out CHOP
text: DMX Out CHOP (CHOP). The DMX Out CHOP sends channels to DMX, Art-Net, sACN, KiNET, or FTDI devices. Channel values for DMX are 0-255. Parameters (38): Active, Interface, Enttec Generic Serial, KiNET Version, DmxOut (v1), Format, Packet Per Sample, Routing Table, Send ArtSync, Device, *, Serial Port, COM1, Rate, Net (0-127), Subnet (0-15), Universe (0-15), Universe, CID, Source, Priority, Custom KiNET Port, KiNET Port, Multicast.

title: DMX Out POP
text: DMX Out POP (POP). The DMX Out POP can send to DMX, Art-Net, sACN, [https://www.colorkinetics.com/global/learn/optics-matter KiNET], or FTDI devices. The DMX Out POP merges the DMX universes from each of its [[DMX Fixture POP]] inputs, and sends them to the device. It can merge any number of DMX Fixture POPs but conflicts may arise if multiple DMX Fixture POPs write to the same channel value of a DMX universe. A warning will be output in that case, and for debugging such conflicts the [[DMX Map DAT]] is a useful tool. '''Note: ''' every input must be a DMX Fixture POP, whether they are wired or by parameter OP reference. The DMX in TouchDesigner was developed on the [http://www.enttec.com ENTTEC] device, namely their [http://www.enttec.com/?main_menu=Products&pn=70304 DMX USB Pro] and DMX over Ethernet devices, but it should work for many devices and software that support DMX/Art-Net/sACN/KiNET. A Routing Table can be provided in a DAT where network addresses can be specified by adding rows for each DMX512 universe and then specifying the net, subnet and universe values. '''Tip''': You can use a single DMX Out POP to gather and serve any number of DMX Fixture POPs in your project because you can specify recursively from root /* the path of all DMX Fixture POPs to Search for in the DMX Fixture POPs parameter. You can use a [[Folder DAT]] to find all the DMX Fixture POPs in your project. '''ENTTEC NOTE:''' - Use ENTTEC's [http://www.enttec.com/us/products/controls/dmx-over-ethernet/nmu/ NMU (Node Management Utility)] to configure and inspect the ENTTEC devices found on your network. '''macOS NOTE:''' - ENTTEC USB Pro may not connect automatically, to enable it enter the following command in the Terminal: sudo kextunload -b com.apple.driver.AppleUSBFTDI '''Tip''': Use [https://www.wireshark.org/ WireShark] to watch your DMX network traffic. When sending to a large number of universes (eg. for volumetric lighting) it is advantageous to use unicast (see: DMXFixtureNetAddress attribute for the [[DMX Fixture POP]]) rather than broadcast as it will be more efficient and can avoid overwhelming the network. See also: [[Art-Net]], [[sACN]], [[DMX In CHOP]], [[DMX Out CHOP]], [[DMX]] Parameters (63): /*, active, interface, serial, rate, fixture, fixture0active, fixture0pop, serialport, com3, dmxkingport, default, device, *, multicast, netaddress, localaddress, 192.168.178.150, localport, customport, netport, priority, sendartsync, cid.

title: Edge TOP

For each pixel, it looks at the values at neighboring pixels, and where differences are greater than a threshold, the output's value is higher. title: Envelope CHOP
text: Envelope CHOP (CHOP). The Envelope CHOP outputs the maximum amplitude in the vicinity of each sample of the input. It takes the absolute value of the input, and uses a sliding window of a number of samples to find the maximum amplitude near each sample. Parameters (19): Type, Exponential Decay, Envelope Bounds, Magnitude, Envelope Width, Envelope Width Unit, Interpolate, None, Normalize Power Envelope, Resample Envelope, Sample Rate, Time Slice, Scope, Sample Rate Match, Resample At First Input's Rate, Export Method, DAT Table by Index, Export Root, Export Table.

title: Environment Light COMP
text: Environment Light COMP (COMP). The Environment Light Component controls the color and intensity of an environmental light in a given scene. This light, unlike the Light Component, has no particular position. It comes from outside all of the objects in the scene and lights them. The orientation of the objects and the Environment Light Map will affect how each side of the objects is lit. However the position of the objects in space will have no effect on how the environment light hits them. Parameters (44): Light Color, Red, Dimmer, Environment Map, Environment Map 2D Type, Equirectangular, Environment Map Quality, Environment Map Rotate, X, Use Pre-Filter Maps, Off, Pre-Filtered Specular Map, Material, Render, Draw Priority, Pick Priority, Wireframe Color, Light Mask, Extension, Object, Name, Promote, Re-Init Extensions, Parent Shortcut.
